subject.title: Geography
subject.description:
**IB Geography** is a **Group 3** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **physical and human geography, environmental interactions, global challenges, and spatial patterns**, with a strong focus on **fieldwork, data analysis, case studies, and Geographic Information Systems (GIS)**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2025**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Geographic themes - structured questions | 2h 15min | 2h 15min | SL: 35% / HL: 35% |
| **Paper 2** | Geographic perspectives - data response and essays | 1h 15min | 1h 15min | SL: 40% / HL: 45% |
| **Paper 3** | Global interactions - extended essay responses | N/A | 1h | HL: 20% |
| **Internal Assessment** | Fieldwork investigation (2,500 words) | 20 hours | 20 hours | SL: 25% / HL: 20% |

strengths: Clear, focused geographical question with explicit links to Bradshaw/Schumm theory
Precise identification of a 14 km stretch of the River LâAsse and ten sampling sites with demonstrated feasibility
Testable hypotheses grounded in theoretical foundations
weaknesses: Mapping lacks coordinate grid, clear titles and consistent conventions
Minor inconsistency in describing the river mouthâs context
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Two strands (question formulation and location specificity) comfortably meet the topâband descriptors. Although mapping conventions have minor lapses (coordinate grid, titles, north arrows), the overall fieldwork question and geographic context are exemplary. Thus, the criterion merits the full 3 marks.
feedback.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

strengths: Stratified sampling strategy clearly explained with rationale
Correct implementation of width and sediment size measurements across multiple sites
weaknesses: Limited number of variables measured (only channel width and clast size)
Absence of flowâmeter protocol and some conflation in sampling description
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Methods are described with clear relevance to the question and correctly implemented, yielding reliable primary data, but the restricted variable set and brevity in certain procedural details limit the depth. This aligns with the midâband descriptor for 2 marks.

strengths: All data presented are directly relevant to the hypotheses, enabling substantive analysis
Appropriate use of proportional symbol maps, scatter and bar graphs with supporting tables
Conventions (titles, legends, north arrows) are mostly adhered to, with only occasional lapses
weaknesses: Incomplete coverage of variables (velocity, depth, discharge missing)
Graphs sometimes lack units and scale keys; photographing conventions (orientation, scale bars) need improvement
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The information is relevant and sufficient for partial to substantial analysis, and presentation techniques are appropriate and varied. Occasional convention errors prevent topâband performance, but the work lies in the upper half of the 3â4 band, justifying 4 marks.
feedback.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

strengths: Descriptive statistics (means, lines of best fit) correctly applied and trends linked to theory
Identification and discussion of anomalies (e.g., contradictory bedload result)
Wellâdeveloped links between geological/landâuse factors and bedload size
weaknesses: Statistical analysis is limited (Chiâsquare test mentioned but not executed; no confidence levels)
Discussion occasionally speculative and dense, reducing clarity
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Analysis employs valid descriptive and limited statistical techniques, links patterns to theory, and discusses anomalies. While statistical depth and execution need strengthening, the work reaches the upper midâband for written analysis, meriting 4 marks.

strengths: Conclusion restates main findings and acknowledges both support and contradiction of hypotheses
weaknesses: Lacks numeric reference or confidence levels to fully substantiate claims
Justification remains brief and largely descriptive
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The conclusion is present and partially supported by the analysis, fitting the lowerâband descriptor. It does not yet reach full support with numerical evidence, hence 1 mark is awarded.

strengths: Outlines relevant strengths and weaknesses such as equipment failure and site selection
Offers improvement suggestions (backup equipment, more sites) with general rationale
weaknesses: Evaluation label is misspelt and lacks methodological validity discussion
Improvement suggestions are brief and without detailed impact analysis
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The evaluation appropriately outlines strengths/weaknesses and suggests relevant improvements, but lacks depth and comprehensive impact explanation. This aligns with the midâband descriptor for 2 marks.

strandSelections.reasoning: Several maps are provided (overall location, tributary, geology/land use, proportional symbol site map) and most include scale, north arrow and legend.  However, some images are low resolution or awkwardly oriented and a few cartographic conventions (title clarity, coordinate grid) are missing.  This places the work in the âmap included but with excess detail / minor convention issuesâ level rather than the fully polished top band.

The student formulates a clear, focused and wholly geographical question centred on downstream changes in fluvial characteristics â a textbook IA river study.  The question is explicitly linked to Bradshaw/Schumm theory and two testable hypotheses are stated, showing that theoretical foundations are understood and will guide subsequent analysis.  This level of relevance and clarity matches the topâband descriptor.

The IA uses proportional symbol mapping, scatter and bar graphs, annotated photographs and tables â all appropriate to river studies and mostly effective.  However, graph formatting is inconsistent (axes sometimes lack units, small fonts) and more sophisticated statistics are absent, so it does not reach the âmost effective techniquesâ description.  Mid-band is justified.

Techniques (tape/ranging pole for width, calliper for clast size) were carried out correctly and repeated at each site, yielding a usable primary dataset.  However, the sample of nine sites actually collected and the limited range of variables restrict reliability, and the student later admits equipment problems.  Thus the work demonstrates correct but fairly minimal implementation â suitable for band 2.

The student employs descriptive statistics (mean values), a line of best fit and identifies outliers; a Chi-square test is mentioned but not executed.  While appropriate descriptive/limited statistical methods are attempted, execution is only partial, consistent with the 3â4 band.

Trends and anomalies for both variables are described and linked to theory (Bradshaw, erosion processes, vegetation/human impact).  The reasoning is generally sound but occasionally speculative, and the contradictory bedload result is explained but not statistically tested.  This meets the descriptor âtrends and anomalies identified and linkedâ, warranting mid-band.

A precise stretch (c.14 km) of the River LâAsse is named; ten specific sampling sites are indicated, and feasibility for primary data collection is demonstrated through photographs and safety discussion.  The narrative gives physical setting, length, source and mouth, satisfying the requirement for a precise, workable location.

The evaluation outlines several relevant strengths and weaknesses (equipment failure, time pressure, site number) and comments on reliability, fulfilling the descriptor of an âappropriate outlineâ.  Depth is limited (no discussion of methodological validity or sampling error magnitude), so the comprehensive top band is not achieved.

Data gathered (channel width and clast size at multiple sites) are relevant to both hypotheses, allowing partial analysis.  Yet coverage is incomplete (only nine sites; no velocity, depth or discharge data), so sufficiency is limited.  This aligns with the 3â4 band where âmost data are relevant allowing partial analysisâ â a score of 3 reflects the lower end of that band.

The conclusion restates findings and partially links them to the evidence, acknowledging support for width and contradiction for bedload.  However, justification remains brief and largely descriptive, lacking numerical reference or confidence levels.  This is a âpartially supported conclusionâ, earning 1 mark.

Analysis addresses both hypotheses, incorporates maps/graphs and discusses outliers, yet velocity/discharge variables promised in theory are missing, and statistical depth is limited.  Therefore analysis is substantive but with clear gaps, fitting the 3â4 band with a score of 3.

Sampling strategy (stratified choice of 10 sites, rationale for rejecting systematic/random) and measurement procedures for width and sediment size are explained with diagrams and theoretical justification.  Nevertheless, only two river variables are addressed and some descriptions remain brief (e.g., no mention of flow-meter protocol despite later evaluation).  Detail is adequate but not âcomprehensiveâ, fitting the mid-band.

Titles, legends and north arrows are generally present, but orientation issues, truncated captions and occasional spelling/formatting errors show convention lapses.  These are âoccasionalâ rather than frequent, situating the work at the 3â4 band with a score of 3.

Improvement suggestions are offered (backup equipment, better organisation, more sites) with brief rationale, but there is little elaboration of how these changes would impact data quality beyond general statements.  This meets the mid-band descriptor of ârelevant improvement suggestions outlinedâ.

annotations.comment: The fieldwork question is clearly formulated and directly linked to Bradshaw/Schumm theory, enabling testable hypotheses and strong geographical focus.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: The paraphrased question here diverges from the exact fieldwork question. Tighten wording to mirror your original formulation and maintain theoretical clarity.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: The map lacks a coordinate grid and clear title on the figure itself. Add these conventions to meet mapping best practices.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: Sampling description conflates stratified and systematic methods. Clarify which approach was used and justify its relevance to your question.
Measurement of width is described but lacks flow-meter protocol. Include detailed steps for velocity measurement to strengthen your methods section.
Hypotheses are concise, testable and directly tied to the research question, showing clear theoretical foundation.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: Justification effectively invokes lateral erosion and Bradshaw/Schumm processes to support hypotheses, showing strong theoretical linkage.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: The upstream/downstream table is incomplete, with many blank cells. Populate both columns fully or clarify relevance to avoid reader confusion.
Location description is inconsistent: the river mouth is alternately given as the RhÃ´ne in Lake Geneva and the Durance. Ensure geographic context is precise and consistent.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: Proportional symbol map omits a legend linking symbol size to actual widths. Include a scale key for clarity.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: Sample size is unclear for sediment measurement. Justify picking three stones per depth and discuss representativeness.
The âQuality and treatment of informationâ header is present but lacks descriptive content on data reliability or validation.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: Graphs lack axis labels and unit annotations. Ensure each chart includes clear labels and units for accurate interpretation.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: The scatter plot lacks statistical detail (e.g., $$R^2$$ value or regression equation). Include these to deepen your analytical rigour.
The tributary map omits a north arrow and scale, reducing clarity of spatial representation.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: Annotated photographs are presented without orientation or scale. Add directional markers and scale bars to improve data presentation.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: A Chi-square test is mentioned but not executed. Either perform the calculation or remove the heading to match your actual analysis.
Channel width interpretation links to human interference without concrete evidence. Anchor explanations with specific site data or external references.
Bedload analysis contradicts your hypothesis and text description. Reconcile graph trends with narrative and verify data interpretation.
Dense exposition here reduces clarity. Consider subdividing discussion by factor (vegetation, geology, urban) and adding subheadings.
Linking of geology and land use to bedload size is well executed, demonstrating thorough contextual understanding.
Conclusion restates findings but lacks numeric support. Incorporate specific data points and confidence to strengthen your conclusion.
The evaluation heading is misspelt as âFvaluationâ. Correct spelling and expand to detail methodology strengths/weaknesses.
Improvement suggestion links flowâmeter issues to general organization. Strengthen by explaining how a backup protocol would enhance data reliability.
Time constraint comments are valid but lack concrete mitigation. Propose specific scheduling or team allocation strategies for future fieldwork.
